CHINA AND THE KOREAN PENINSULA
A Chinese View
Hao Yufan
History and geography have combined to make the
Korean peninsula vitally important to China's security. This importance
lies not only in the fact that the peninsula shares a fairly long border with
China's industrial heartland in the northeastern provinces, but it also
stems from the convergence—and often the clash—of the interests of the
Soviet Union, Japan, and the United States in Korea. For the last century,
Korea has served as an object or area of conflict and an invasion corridor
for these three powerful states. The Chinese involvement in the Korean
War in 1950-53 in support of North Korea after the United States inter-
vened on behalf of South Korea, together with the close ties between the
leadership of the Chinese Communist Party and the Korean Workers
Party led by Kim Il Sung that dates from the 1930s, have reinforced the
importance of Korea in China's policy calculations.
Since the founding of the People's Republic of China (PRC), Beijing's
Korean policy has been strongly influenced by its overall security con-
cerns. This policy has basically gone through three periods, with China-
North Korean relations as its cornerstone. In the 1950s, China saw the
United States as the major threat to its security and it allied itself with the
Soviet Union and supported the Democratic People's Republic of Korea
(DPRK) against the American-backed South Korean regime. From the
early 1960s to the 1970s, while the United States remained a security
threat, the Sino-Soviet dispute created a new threat that included the dan-
ger the DPRK might become an exclusive ally of Moscow, unfriendly to
China. Chinese regional policy was therefore twofold: to support the

CHINA AND THE KOREAN PENINSULA
A Chinese View
Hao Yufan
History and geography have combined to make the
Korean peninsula vitally important to China's security. This importance
lies not only in the fact that the peninsula shares a fairly long border with
China's industrial heartland in the northeastern provinces, but it also
stems from the convergence—and often the clash—of the interests of the
Soviet Union, Japan, and the United States in Korea. For the last century,
Korea has served as an object or area of conflict and an invasion corridor
for these three powerful states. The Chinese involvement in the Korean
War in 1950-53 in support of North Korea after the United States inter-
vened on behalf of South Korea, together with the close ties between the
leadership of the Chinese Communist Party and the Korean Workers
Party led by Kim Il Sung that dates from the 1930s, have reinforced the
importance of Korea in China's policy calculations.
Since the founding of the People's Republic of China (PRC), Beijing's
Korean policy has been strongly influenced by its overall security con-
cerns. This policy has basically gone through three periods, with China-
North Korean relations as its cornerstone. In the 1950s, China saw the
United States as the major threat to its security and it allied itself with the
Soviet Union and supported the Democratic People's Republic of Korea
(DPRK) against the American-backed South Korean regime. From the
early 1960s to the 1970s, while the United States remained a security
threat, the Sino-Soviet dispute created a new threat that included the dan-
ger the DPRK might become an exclusive ally of Moscow, unfriendly to
China. Chinese regional policy was therefore twofold: to support the
DPRK in the struggle against U.S.-backed South Korea on the one hand,
and to dissuade Kim I Sung from taking the Soviet side in the Sino-Soviet
rivalry. Throughout the 1970s, China's first priority was to deal with an
increasing Soviet threat to its security. For this purpose, China developed
a new relationship with the United States and Japan. At the same time,
Beijing carefully maintained its relations with the DPRK, though not
without difficulties.1

rivalry. Throughout the 1970s, China's first priority was to deal with an
increasing Soviet threat to its security. For this purpose, China developed
a new relationship with the United States and Japan. At the same time,
Beijing carefully maintained its relations with the DPRK, though not
without difficulties.1
Since the beginning of the 1980s, the security environment of the North-
east Asian region surrounding the Korean peninsula has been undergoing
significant change and has become fluid. All the major powers still seem to
support the common, minimum objective of maintaining the stability of
the region. There remain, however, many uncertainties involving this sen-
sitive and heavily armed area. The fluid and dynamic situation poses a
serious challenge to China's regional objectives and has attiacted more and
more attention from Beijing's foreign policy makers. What are the impor-
tant recent changes in and around the Korean peninsula? What will be
China's Korean policy in the remainder of the 1980s? This essay attempts
to answer these questions by examining two major interrelated factors that
play a pivotal role in shaping China's Korean policy: (1) the changing re-
gional situation; and (2) China's fundamental policy objectives in the area.
The essay will analyze trends that seem to be of particular significance for
Chinese interests, and will discuss how the changing strategic environment
has affected and will continue to affect Beijing's pursuit of its regional in-
terests.
Trends In and Around the Korean
Peninsula
Chinese policy toward Korea is largely influenced by the security environ-
ment in and around the peninsula. The situation in the area could be re-
garded as a kind of balance of power or equilibrium in which the six
parties constantly interact and adjust their relationships, each trying not
only to maintain and improve its position but also to prevent a dangerous
new imbalance from emerging.
Since the 1980s began, challenges to the regional equilibrium have been
looming on the horizon. Four trends seem particularly significant for the

The Soviet Military Buildup
The first and most significant trend that has challenged the balanced situa-
tion is the rapid increase in Soviet military capabilities. The Soviets believe
that the existing balance of power in this region has been more or less
unfavorable to them since the 1970s, and they are determined to improve
their current position by increasing their own influence and power there.
The Soviet Union's Far East policy has had three main ingredients since
the 1970s: 1) to challenge the United States in East Asia; 2) to contain
China in an effort to keep China weak; and 3) to discourage Japan
from
becoming a strong military power, and to prevent any Sino-U.S.-Japanese
entente against the USSR. This policy was not very successful in the
1970s.2 China moved into a de facto, if very loose, cooperation with the
United States and Japan. Subsequently, it has begun a modernization pro-
gram which, by the year 2000, could significantly increase its power.
America's relations with East Asia have never been better. It has an ex-
tensive alliance system in East Asia, and has for the first time in this cen-
tury maintained good relations with both China and Japan.
Thus, the Soviets believed that a rapid buildup of their military and
arms assistance capability would be a major means to change their unfa-
vorable status. Actually, Moscow started its buildup in the late 1960s, and
it has basically undergone two stages: the first, from the late 1960s to the
early 1970s, emphasized the rapid buildup of ground forces deployed pri-
marily against China. The second stage involved the deployment of a new
generation of intermediate-range nuclear weapons, the major expansion
and qualitative improvement of its Pacific Fleet, and the development and
extension of Soviet bases in the territories north of Japan. Its objective
began to include a military capability to counter U.S. air and naval deploy-
ment in the Pacific and to interdict the sea and air lines of communication
linking the United States with the region in order to neutralize the poten-
tial for U.S.-China-Japan cooperation.

of its ICBM force and ballis-

of its ground forces and fighter air-

of its strategic bombers and navy forces.3
Moreover, these forces now have been equipped with some of the most
modern weapons in the Soviet inventory, including a Kiev-class aircraft
carrier displacing 40,000 tons (the Minsk), an amphibious assault ship (the
Ivan Rogov), Delta-class SSNBs, MiG-25 "Foxbat" fighters, MiG-27
"Floggers", Tu-22M "Backfire" bombers, Tu-95s ("Bear/E"), and SS-20
IRBMs.4 This buildup in East Asia has taken the Soviet Union a consider-
able way along the road toward its goal of altering the balance in its favor.
In the process, it has begun to change the basic structure of power in the
region.5
Although Soviet leaders for the last decade have behaved as if they as-
sign the Korean peninsula a low priority in their overall East Asian policy,
this attitude has undergone a gradual change recently.6 The Sea of
Okhotsk has become much more important to the USSR as a bastion for
SSBNs whose long-range missiles have been targeted on the U.S. since the
early 1980s. Over the same period, the Soviets have been steadily aug-
menting the role of Northeast Asia as a platform and staging area for the
wartime deployment southward, to the Cam Ranh Bay base in Vietnam
and the Indian Ocean.
Given the growing geostrategic importance of Northeast Asia to Mos-
cow, and the fundamental and long-term nature of its competition with the
United States and China, the Soviets are not likely to change this current
policy orientation. A strong military presence and capability in the region
have simply become too important for Moscow to neglect. Therefore, the
Soviet buildup in the region is likely to remain a constant feature of the
strategic environment through the 1980s. Although the magnitude and
speed of the buildup may vary, it seems unlikely that the Soviets will halt,
let alone reverse, the process.
China's interests certainly are not served by this Soviet buildup in
Northeast Asia. First of all, the qualitative and quantitative military
buildup increases the threat to China's security. Second, it could threaten
the regional power equilibrium and affect the quadrilateral relationship
among the U.S., Japan, China, and the USSR, and the balance of forces
between North and South Korea. Third, it might also increase the possi-
bility of major Japanese rearmament efforts, which also would be undesir-

The Improvement of Soviet-DPRK Relations
Related to the rise of Soviet expansion in East Asia is a trend toward im-
provement of relations between the USSR and the DPRK.7
Over the
years this relationship has fluctuated widely: from extremely close in the
early 1950s, to an almost total break in the early 1960s, with variations
between the two extremes from the mid-1960s to the early 1980s. These
fuctuations indicate some divergence of interests and difficulties in manag-
ing differences in their bilateral relations, as well as the mutual distrust
imbedded therein. However, strategic needs, as perceived by both sides,
have held the two countries together.
The USSR's interests in North Korea are primarily security concerns
defined in terms of the Soviet global and regional perspective.
The
strengthened U.S.-Japan and U.S.-South Korean security alliances and the
possibility of U.S.-Japan-China cooperation directed against the USSR
have augmented the strategic importance of North Korea to the Soviets.
Its economic interests there are marginal, but its political interests demand
that Pyongyang not side with China in the Sino-Soviet rivalry. For North
Korea, the Soviet Union is the principal source of economic and military
aid and the guarantor, though not a totally reliable one, of its security in
the face of the external threat. Pyongyang hopes, while maintaining its
independence, that Moscow will lend full support in its unification and
political struggle against the South.
Throughout the 1970s, Pyongyang tilted markedly to Beijing rather
than Moscow. It published China's attacks on the USSR, criticized Cuba,
condemned Vietnam, and denounced "dominationism," which was clearly
aimed at the Soviet Union. At the same time, Kim Il Sung has always held
open the possibility of improving relations with Moscow, and has wanted
to demonstrate North Korea's independence of Beijing. In the context of
Beijing-Pyongyang strains in 1979-80, Kim Il Sung began to make cau-
tious overtures to the Soviets, signalling a desire for improved bilateral
relations, but the Soviets showed little response until after Brezhnev's
death in late 1982. The Korean Airlines incident and the Rangoon bomb-
ing in 1983 provided opportunities for the two to move closer together.

relations, but the Soviets showed little response until after Brezhnev's
death in late 1982. The Korean Airlines incident and the Rangoon bomb-
ing in 1983 provided opportunities for the two to move closer together.
When a Soviet fighter plane shot down the KAL airliner, Pyongyang, after
three weeks of silence, endorsed the Moscow explanation of the incident,
blaming the United States. Shortly thereafter the Rangoon bombing oc-
curred, and the Soviets published Pyongyang's defense of its position and
ignored the official Burmese account.8

North Korean-Soviet relations have seen a steady and substantial im-
provement since Kim Il Sung's official visit to the USSR in May 1984.
Although there were no immediately visible benefits, Pyongyang began to
change its attitude toward the Soviet Union. One remarkable change was
Pyongyang's willingness to express gratitude to the Soviets for the defeat
of Japan and liberation of Korea. For years, the Soviets had felt aggrieved
because the North Koreans had attributed the liberation of Korea exclu-
sively to the efforts of the Korean People's Revolutionary Army led by
Kim Il Sung. On Liberation Day in 1984, Kim, for the first time in many
years, praised the "liberating role played by the Soviet Union."9 Through
this expression of gratitude, Pyongyang attempted to remove an irritating
and emotional obstacle in its relations with Moscow.
In November 1984, Soviet Deputy Foreign Minister Mikhail Kapitsa
made a 13-day visit to Pyongyang to negotiate a new border treaty, which
was signed in Moscow in April 1985.
This treaty apparently gave both
countries better access to each other's territory, economically and milita-
rily. As a result, the Soviets agreed to supply North Korea with advanced
MiG-23 fighter aircraft.10 Recent Soviet aircraft overflights across the
narrow neck of North Korea's east-west corridor along the DMZ, may
also be a result of the border treaty. Soviet First Deputy Premier Geydar
Aliyev went to Pyongyang in August 1985 to attend the celebration of the
40th Anniversary of Korea's Liberation. He was accompanied by First
Deputy Minister of Defense Marshal V. Petrov, who reportedly offered
SAM-3 missiles to North Korea. It is worth noting that Pyongyang did
not invite China to attend the ceremony, a clear indication of North Ko-
rea's tilt toward the Soviets. To underscore the warming of Soviet-North
Korean relations and military cooperation, three Soviet warships, headed
by the flagship Tallin with the first deputy commander of the Soviet Pacific
Fleet aboard, visited Wonsan, the first Soviet navy port visit to North Ko-
rea.11

Why did Moscow-Pyongyang relations improve so dramatically in the
1984-85 period? For Pyongyang, trends in Chinese domestic and foreign

China's Current Objectives and Possible
Approaches
The trends described above are the most significant factors affecting the
security environment in Northeast Asia as far as regional stability is con-
cerned. These trends have two basic features: the rising Soviet power and
influence and the increase in destabilizing factors. In the long run, these
are moving in a direction not congenial to Chinese interests. What are
China's regional objectives, and how will China respond in the context of
these trends in the remainder of the 1980s? China's policy toward the
Korean peninsula is largely a function of its overall foreign policy con-
cerns, which at present are based on the following premises:
(1) The Soviet Union is the main threat to China's security. From the Chi-
nese perspective, the center of Moscow's Asian policy is to strategically encircle
and weaken China.
The steady growth of Soviet ground, air, naval, and strate-
gic forces deployed in East Asia, together with Soviet success in expanding its
influence in Indochina and Afghanistan, pose a major challenge to the PRC's
security interests.
()Despite this major threat from the Soviet Union, military conflict with

These foreign policy calculations require China to pursue an independ-

policy toward the Korean peninsula to be aimed at three basic objectives:
First, to maintain regional stability and the existing balance. Any develop-

Conclusion: Future PRC Policy
In light of China's objectives and possible approaches and the possible
trends described above, it would be desirable for Beijing's policy toward
Korea and Northeast Asia to be based on the following judgments and to
move in the following directions:
China should be concerned but not excessively alarmed by the Soviet
buildup in Northeast Asia. It certainly does not serve China's interest.
Yet, China should not worry too much about it since the major aim of the
Soviet buildup is to counter the American military presence in this re-
gion—although it also is a result of Soviet fear of potential U.S.-China-
Japan cooperation. China seems to have at least two options in responding
to this Soviet buildup. The first is to moderately improve relations with
Moscow and distance it from the concept of U.S.-Japan-PRC military co-
operation. This might help reduce tension in this region, although it may
also entail dangers that are inherent in appeasement. Another response

In the coming decade, Japan will become increasingly important to
China. China and Japan share common apprehensions about the growing
Soviet military buildup in East Asia and have the same interests in main-
taining peace and stability in the Korean peninsula. However, Beijing-
Tokyo relations have not been devoid of problems, including trade dis-
putes, the Japanese textbook issue, and the controversy over island terri-
tory. The memory of the Japanese invasion of China will continue to
make Beijing wary of any signs of a revival of militarism, and Tokyo will
prefer that China continue to place a low priority on military moderniza-
tion. China will continue to pursue a policy of close cooperation with Ja-
pan, not only to contain Soviet expansion but also to support China's
economic modernization program. Beijing will continue its efforts to solve
problems in its relations with Japan by frequent high-level consultations
and by mutual compromise. At the same time, the PRC leadership will
continue to hint to the Japanese that China does not want to see Japan
become a military power and that any resurgence of militarism will evoke
a reaction from the Chinese.40
Conclusion: Future PRC Policy
In light of China's objectives and possible approaches and the possible
trends described above, it would be desirable for Beijing's policy toward
Korea and Northeast Asia to be based on the following judgments and to
move in the following directions:
China should be concerned but not excessively alarmed by the Soviet
buildup in Northeast Asia. It certainly does not serve China's interest.
Yet, China should not worry too much about it since the major aim of the
Soviet buildup is to counter the American military presence in this re-
gion—although it also is a result of Soviet fear of potential U.S.-China-
Japan cooperation. China seems to have at least two options in responding
to this Soviet buildup. The first is to moderately improve relations with
Moscow and distance it from the concept of U.S.-Japan-PRC military co-
operation. This might help reduce tension in this region, although it may
also entail dangers that are inherent in appeasement. Another response
would be to increase cooperative ties with the U.S. and Japan. This would
make efforts to counter Soviet expansion more effective and might even
impel the Soviets to reevaluate their East Asian policy. Some observers
interpret Gorbachev's recent overture to China as the result of the fear of
this possibility.41 Although currently remaining closer to the United
States than to the Soviet Union, China seems now to hesitate in making a
basic decision and is more or less in the middle between the United States
and the USSR.

The "three obstacles" in Sino-Soviet relations should be regarded as a
touchstone indicating the Kremlin's real intentions toward Beijing. If the
Soviets change their anti-China policy by removing the three obstacles,
China could cautiously choose the option of distancing itself from Wash-
ington and seeking an equidistant position between the superpowers.
However, if these conditions were to be only partially met, the Soviet
Union would still constitute the primary threat to China's security, and
China should still maintain its close cooperative relationship with the
United States. Given the basic, long-term competition between the U.S.
and the USSR and the pressing internal economic problems perceived in
Beijing, Moscow has incentives to seek relief in its relations with Beijing or
at least to partially prevent China from forming closer ties with Washing-
ton. Therefore, China should try to direct the current situation in ways
that are less potentially explosive and more mutually beneficial. China will
see its interests best served by an easing of tension with Moscow, step by
step, and at the same time maintaining good relations with Washington.
This would further improve China's already favorable position in the U.S.-
Soviet-PRC triangle, and would be consistent with the thrust of China's
policy interests in the Korean peninsula.
Sino-American relations will continue to be influenced by the Soviet fac-
tor. The attempt to deal with the expansion of Soviet military power in the
region is likely to preoccupy American leaders for some years to come.
Because of the perceived cost to the United States of dealing with the Sovi-

allies. Thus, China's value receives a special twist: if the PRC could be
counted on to stand up to the Soviet challenge, it would spare America
from having to bear the brunt of the fighting in the event of any military
conflict. In this context, the United States can be expected to compromise

its relations with Beijing so as to strengthen its efforts to contain Soviet

The improvement of Soviet-North Korean relations demands special at-
tention, but China should recognize the limits in this relationship. In spite
of his disillusionment with some of China's current policies, Kim I Sung is
highly unlikely to tilt totally toward Moscow, mainly because of his sense
of independence, his view that the chuch'e idea represents North Korea's
fundamental national interests, and also because of the deep-rooted diver-
gence of interests and distrust between Moscow and Pyongyang. Among
fundamental North Korean aspirations, the desire for independence has
received the top priority; it is rooted in Korea's historical experience.
North Korea thus will resist Soviet efforts at de facto integration. Chinese
sensitivity, and Soviet insensitivity, to North Korea's desire for indepen-
dence has contributed to the character of Pyongyang-Moscow and Pyong-
yang-Beijing relations.42
The Soviets always find the North Koreans intractable, ungrateful, and
difficult to manage. Pyongyang has often gone contrary to Soviet wishes
and sometimes openly attacked Moscow's policies. But because of the
strategic importance of North Korea, Moscow cannot treat Pyongyang
harshly. The current North Korean leadership sees the USSR as a big,
threatening neighbor that would like to dominate North Korea as it does
Mongolia. The North Korean leaders also recognize that they must de-
pend on the Soviets to some extent, economically and militarily, and more
importantly as the principal counter to U.S.-backed South Korea. This
unavoidable dependence breeds frustration and resentment.43 Moscow
keeps North Korea on a short rein, providing neither the military nor the
economic support that it would like to have. Moscow's reliability is also
questionable; the historical lesson of the Korean War is still fresh in the
minds of the DPRK's leadership: It was the Chinese not the Soviets that
came to the defense of the DPRK. These factors resulted in DPRK-Soviet
bilateral relations remaining cool, and sometimes strained, for most of the
last two decades and they are likely to create obstacles to close relations in
the future.

trend might have three beneficial implications for Beijing. First, it could
reduce the Soviet fear of being surrounded by a new containment system
and excluded from peninsular affairs and thus compelled to resort to pro-

Sino-Soviet tensions, this concern has subsided considerably. Second, it
would encourage Pyongyang to continue its independent policy, which
serves Chinese interests better than its dependence on either Moscow or
Beijing. As a vital buffer to Soviet expansion, an independent North Ko-

might be helpful if the Soviets do preserve some influence and help to pre-

China should try to avoid serving as a host, mediator, or chairman in
any tripartite negotiations. If China can promote the idea of tripartite
talks and accomplish some tangible results on the DPRK's behalf, this
could help to solidify Chinese relations with North Korea. If, however,
China fails to do this, it could reduce China's credibility and utility in the
eyes of the North Koreans, who will see clearly the limits of China's diplo-
matic abilities as well as Pyongyang of the Sino-American detente, and
might then tilt toward the Soviets for diplomatic support.
Although until very recently China has lagged behind the Soviet Union
in semi-official dealings with South Korea, it is clear that Pyongyang is
extremely sensitive to Chinese openings toward South Korea. This sensi-
tivity in Pyongyang reveals the importance of China in the DPRK's policy
calculations, which China should appreciate and value. China's economic
contacts with Seoul should not be expanded to the extent that PRC-
DPRK relations might be compromised. North Korea should always be a
higher priority. China should not change its opposition to all "two
Koreas" formulas—such as "cross-recognition" and the "simultaneous ad-
mission" to the United Nations. If Pyongyang modifies its position on
these issues, so can China. China should be careful about dealing officially
with Seoul. Any formal official contact might be regarded as an indication
of China's willingness to accept the "two Koreas" idea, which in turn
could be viewed in some countries as strengthening the case for "two Chi-
nas". Seoul has openly indicated that if the PRC deals with South Korea
diplomatically, it would sever its relations with Taipei. Although this
would further the PRC's efforts to isolate Taiwan in the world community,
the cost would surpass the benefits.
